---
title: "A flexible modelling framework for estimating thermal tolerance and sensitivity"
shorttitle: "Bayesian models of thermal tolerance"
authors:
  - name: "Daniel W.A. Noble"
    email: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Pieter A. Arnold"
    affiliations:
      - ref: anu
  - name: "Shinichi Nakagawa"
    affiliations:
      - ref: ualberta
  - name: "Patrice Pottier"
    email: patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - ref: gu
      - ref: unsw
affiliations:
  - id: anu
    name: Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University
    city: Canberra
    country: Australia
  - id: gu
    name: Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg
    city: Gothenburg
    country: Sweden
  - id: unsw
    name: Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales
    city: Sydney
    state: New South Wales
    country: Australia
  - id: ualberta
    name: Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta
    city: Edmonton
    state: Alberta
    country: Canada
abstract: |
  Extreme heat events are becoming more frequent, intense and prolonged, increasing the need for models that predict impacts on organismal fitness. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models provide this link, but conventional two-stage analyses often discard uncertainty, mishandle censored or overdispersed data and limit hierarchical inference and prediction. We derive equivalences showing how the four-parameter logistic model can recover TDT/TLS quantities, including thermal sensitivity ($z$), critical maxima ($CT_{max}$), $T_{crit}$, heat injury and survival, from one fitted model. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. We introduce `bayesTLS`, an R package for count/proportion data. We use case studies across plant and animal taxa to demonstrate its power for estimating group contrasts and predicting survival under field temperature-time series. This framework provides robust inference and flexible tools for predicting organismal responses to future extreme heat events.
keywords:
  - thermal tolerance landscape
  - dose-response
  - hierarchical Bayesian model
  - 4-parameter logistic
  - critical thermal maximum
  - ectotherm
  - heat tolerance
  - climate change
  - climate vulnerability
  - fluctuating temperature regimes
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    fig-cap-location: bottom
  docx:
    reference-doc: ../bib/templates/template.docx
    toc: false
    number-sections: false

    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    fig-pos: "H"
    colorlinks: true
    link-citations: true
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        % tinytable LaTeX dependencies. See supplement.qmd for rationale.
        \usepackage{tabularray}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{graphicx}
        \usepackage[normalem]{ulem}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayUnderline}[1]{\underline{#1}}
        \providecommand{\tinytableTabularrayStrikeout}[1]{\sout{#1}}
        % Author block: Quarto's default \author{names \and …} gives
        % names only. We override with authblk to pin affiliations from
        % YAML to the title page.
        \usepackage[affil-it]{authblk}
        \renewcommand\Authsep{, }
        \renewcommand\Authand{, }
        \renewcommand\Authands{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\itshape\small}
        \let\OldAuthor\author
        \renewcommand{\author}[1]{}
        \AtBeginDocument{%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Daniel W.A. Noble\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{daniel.noble@anu.edu.au}}}%
          \OldAuthor[1]{Pieter A. Arnold}%
          \OldAuthor[2]{Shinichi Nakagawa}%
          \OldAuthor[3,4]{Patrice Pottier\thanks{Corresponding author: \texttt{patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se}}}%
          \affil[1]{Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia}%
          \affil[2]{Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada}%
          \affil[3]{Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden}%
          \affil[4]{Evolution \& Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia}%
        }
bibliography: ../bib/tdt_problems.bib
csl: ../bib/ecology-letters.csl
link-citations: true
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  cache: true
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
**Daniel W.A. Noble**^1,\*^, **Pieter A. Arnold**^1^, **Shinichi Nakagawa**^2^, **Patrice Pottier**^3,4,\*^

^1^Division of Ecology and Evolution, Research School of Biology, Australian National University, Canberra, Australia.

^2^Department of Biological Sciences, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Canada.

^3^Department of Biological and Environmental Sciences, University of Gothenburg, Gothenburg, Sweden.

^4^Evolution & Ecology Research Centre, School of Biological, Earth and Environmental Sciences, University of New South Wales, Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.

^\*^Corresponding authors: daniel.noble@anu.edu.au; patrice.pottier@bioenv.gu.se

**Abstract**

Extreme heat events are becoming more frequent, intense and prolonged, increasing the need for models that predict impacts on organismal fitness. Thermal death time (TDT) and thermal load sensitivity (TLS) models provide this link, but conventional two-stage analyses often discard uncertainty, mishandle censored or overdispersed data and limit hierarchical inference and prediction. We derive equivalences showing how the four-parameter logistic model can recover TDT/TLS quantities, including thermal sensitivity ($z$), critical maxima ($CT_{max}$), $T_{crit}$, heat injury and survival, from one fitted model. Simulations show the joint model reproduces classical estimates when two-stage assumptions hold and is more reliable when they fail. We introduce `bayesTLS`, an R package for count/proportion data. We use case studies across plant and animal taxa to demonstrate its power for estimating group contrasts and predicting survival under field temperature-time series. This framework provides robust inference and flexible tools for predicting organismal responses to future extreme heat events.

**Keywords:** thermal tolerance landscape; dose-response; hierarchical Bayesian model; 4-parameter logistic; critical thermal maximum; ectotherm; heat tolerance; climate change; climate vulnerability; fluctuating temperature regimes
:::

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| cache: false

# Project-wide objects loaded for inline reporting throughout the manuscript.
# Every number, table, and figure is produced from code — no magic numbers.

library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(ggplot2)
library(brms)
library(posterior)
library(tinytable)

# Force tinytable to register its LaTeX preamble dependencies on every
# render. See supplement.qmd setup-supp for explanation.
invisible(tinytable::tt(data.frame(x = 1)))

set.seed(123)

# Shared figure theme: keep all manuscript figures visually consistent.
# Define once here; load via source() into every figure chunk.
# source(here::here("R", "theme_ms.R"))

# Per-group z / CTmax medians written by ms/supplement.qmd's case-study summary
# build (output/data/fig4_summary.rds; the file name is a stable identifier).
# Used for inline reporting so headline numbers are produced by code, not typed.
# Same render dependency as the case-study and heat-injury figure PNGs: render
# the supplement first to (re)generate it.
fig4_summary <- readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig4_summary.rds"))

# CTmax_1hr across the 1-hour-reference case studies (zebrafish x oxygen, aphid
# species, leaf PSII; the vinegar fly is reported at 4 h and excluded here).
ctmax1h    <- fig4_summary$median[fig4_summary$quantity == "CTmax_1hr"]
ctmax1h_lo <- round(min(ctmax1h), 1)
ctmax1h_hi <- round(max(ctmax1h), 1)

# Zebrafish oxygen contrast (OCLTT): CTmax_1hr under normoxia vs hyperoxia.
.zf_ct <- fig4_summary[fig4_summary$case_study == "Zebrafish (oxygen)" &
                       fig4_summary$quantity == "CTmax_1hr", ]
zf_ctmax_norm  <- round(.zf_ct$median[.zf_ct$group == "normoxia"], 1)
zf_ctmax_hyper <- round(.zf_ct$median[.zf_ct$group == "hyperoxia"], 1)
```

# Introduction

Climate change is increasing the frequency, intensity, and duration of extreme heat events globally, with cascading consequences for organismal fitness, population persistence, and biodiversity [@snook_fertility_2026; @arnold_thermal_2025; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @smale_marine_2019; @wiens_localextinctions_2016]. Quantifying variation in thermal tolerance within and among species is central to forecasting biological responses to climate change in nature [@sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019; @jorgensen_nature_2022]. Threshold-based measures of heat tolerance, such as the critical thermal maximum ($CT_{max}$), have long been used to establish differences among taxa and predict vulnerability to climate change [@pottier_amphibians_2025; @sunday_global_2011; @deutsch_latitude_2008; @sunday_redistribution_2012; @pinsky_marine_2019]. While useful, threshold-based metrics do not capture how the biological effects of heat stress on fitness depend on both exposure duration and intensity, limiting our ability to make realistic predictions in nature [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021].

The Thermal Death Time (TDT) and Thermal Load Sensitivity (TLS) frameworks address this limitation by quantifying how the intensity and duration of heat exposure jointly determine survival and other fitness-related responses [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_nature_2022; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @arnold_thermal_2025]. Recent extensions apply the same logic to sublethal endpoints, such as sterility and heat coma [@orsted_suzukii_2024; @arnold_thermal_2025]. These models describe heat injury as a dose-dependent process above a critical threshold ($T_{crit}$): injury accumulates more rapidly at higher temperatures and during longer exposures, and organisms may eventually reach a thermal load that compromises growth, reproduction, or survival if injury cannot be repaired [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_landscapes_2014; @arnold_thermal_2025; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @buckley_damage_2025; @jorgensen_unifying_2021]. By linking biological responses to the intensity and duration of heat stress, TDT/TLS frameworks allow more realistic predictions under fluctuating thermal regimes [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022; @jorgensen_unifying_2021], and have shown promise in predicting observed mortality rates in nature [@rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @zamora2026].

Despite their predictive power, TDT/TLS analyses traditionally involve a two-stage modelling approach [@orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020; @truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023]. First, organisms are exposed to multiple temperatures for different durations, and the proportion of individuals that are dead, infertile, knocked down, or otherwise affected is modelled as a function of exposure duration. This is typically done by fitting independent dose-response curves at each assay temperature ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]a). From these curves, the exposure duration required to reach a specified response threshold is estimated, most often $t_{50}$, the time required to reach 50% mortality, infertility, knockdown, or some other endpoint. Second, the resulting $\log_{10}(t_{50})$ estimates are regressed against assay temperature using linear regression. Thermal sensitivity ($z$), the temperature increase required to reduce $t_{50}$ by a factor of 10, and critical thermal maxima ($CT_{max_t}$) are then derived from this linear model [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @orsted_finding_2022] ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]b). Because $CT_{max_t}$ is estimated at a fixed exposure duration, it corresponds to the static critical thermal maximum often denoted $sCT_{max}$ in TDT/TLS studies [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @orsted_finding_2022]. We use $CT_{max}$ throughout for brevity. $CT_{max_t}$ is usually computed for a 1-min [@rezende_landscapes_2014], 1-hour [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019], or 4-hour [@orsted_suzukii_2024] duration of heat stress exposure. These quantities can then be used to estimate cumulative Heat Injury (HI) under natural temperatures, where injury accumulates as a function of the exposure duration relative to the predicted tolerance time at each temperature [@jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @villeneuve_dynamic_2024]. Recent extensions have emphasized that injury accumulation may also be offset by repair or acclimation mechanisms, motivating the emergence of new models that incorporate damage-repair dynamics [@arnold_thermal_2025; @buckley_damage_2025; @orsted2026].

While the two-stage pipeline is intuitive and easy to implement, it has several practical and statistical limitations [for a recent discussion of some issues, see @bullard2026]. First, it rarely propagates uncertainty fully. The $t_{50}$ estimates derived in the first stage have non-negligible sampling variance, but they are commonly treated as fixed point estimates in the second-stage regression. Neglecting this sampling variance can bias or miscalibrate inference on $z$ and $CT_{max}$, and lead to inflated $R^2$ values. Second, estimating $t_{50}$ requires data spanning enough of the response curve to anchor the upper and lower asymptotes at each assay temperature. When data are sparse or do not span the response range, $t_{50}$ estimates can be biased or undefined, leading to biased $z$ and $CT_{max}$ estimates and miscalibrated uncertainty. Third, group comparisons between species, sexes, or life stages inherit miscalibrated uncertainty [@lappi2018], which can affect inferences. Reducing each dose-response curve to a fixed $t_{50}$ estimate also makes it difficult to determine which aspects of the dose-response curve are affected. Fourth, fitting separate dose-response models for each assay temperature assumes that these estimates are independent, and typically ignores the hierarchical structure of experimental data (e.g., individuals nested within vials, plates, tanks, clutches, batches, populations, or experimental days). Ignoring this nesting structure prevents the decomposition of variance and can lead to pseudoreplication, particularly when replicate-level variation affects the shape or position of the dose-response curve [@bullard2026]. Finally, the two-stage pipeline does not easily allow downstream predictions of heat injury and survival in nature with uncertainty, which is key to inferring confidence in predictions of extreme-heat impacts.

Here, we present a single-stage alternative to the classical two-stage pipeline: a hierarchical Bayesian four-parameter logistic model fitted jointly to all temperature-by-duration response data. We show how classical TDT/TLS quantities, such as thermal sensitivity ($z$), thermal tolerance ($CT_{max}$), critical temperature thresholds ($T_{crit}$), heat injury accumulation, survival under fluctuating thermal regimes, and their uncertainty, can be derived from this one model. We develop these tools into the R package `bayesTLS` and use simulations and empirical case studies across plant and animal taxa to demonstrate how to implement, validate, and extend this modelling framework.

```{r}
#| label: fig-joint-4pl-conceptual
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Conceptual comparison of the classical two-stage TDT/TLS workflow and the joint Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model. (a) In the two-stage workflow, separate dose-response curves are fit at each assay temperature and the resulting $t_{50}$ estimates are regressed against temperature. This workflow can fail when assays do not bracket $t_{50}$, treats first-stage estimates as fixed and independent, and does not naturally propagate uncertainty, covariates, or hierarchical structure into $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$. (b) The joint 4PL model estimates the full survival surface in one model. Coloured curves show posterior median survival probabilities at assay temperatures, with shaded pointwise 95% credible intervals. Filled circles mark the model midpoint, `mid(T)`, defining relative $\\log_{10}t_{50}$; open triangles mark the absolute 50% survival crossing. The black and steelblue trajectories give the relative and absolute $t_{50}$ relationships used to derive $z_{rel}$, $z_{abs}$, and $CT_{max,1h}$ at the 60-min reference. Dashed ghost curves show the fitted dose response at the corresponding relative and absolute $CT_{max,1h}$ values. Inset: the 4PL parameters `low`, `up`, `mid`, and `k`. Values are illustrative."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "65%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig1.png"))
```

# A non-linear Bayesian hierarchical model for characterizing thermal load sensitivity

## Model specification

Assuming we have mortality data for different times and temperatures, let $y$ denote the number of survivors out of $n$ individuals exposed for time $t$ (e.g., minutes) at assay temperature $T$ (°C). We can model the counts directly with a binomial likelihood (but a beta-binomial likelihood function can also be used and controls for overdispersion):

$$
y \mid n, p \;\sim\; \text{Binomial}(n,\, p),
$$ {#eq-likelihood}

with survival probability $p$ given by a four-parameter logistic (4PL) function of $\log_{10}$-exposure time,

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\bigl(k\,(\log_{10} t - \text{mid})\bigr)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl}

where $\text{low}$ is the lower survival probability asymptote, $\text{up}$ is the upper survival probability asymptote, and $k$ is the slope defining the change in survival probability on the $\log_{10} t$ axis. Larger $k$ gives a sharper decline in survival probability with $\log_{10} t$. $\text{mid}$ is the value of $\log_{10} t$ at which $p$ falls to the midpoint between $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$. The four-parameter logistic model provides a flexible sigmoidal description of thermal dose responses, with interpretable parameters for the lower asymptote, upper asymptote, curve steepness, and midpoint [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_ecotox_2010; @nielsen_nlmm_2004; @rudemo_random_1989; @ritz_streibig_2008]. We focus on binomial and beta-binomial outcomes because count data dominate TDT/TLS studies, but the same modelling logic can be extended to other response types using appropriate likelihoods, including continuous performance traits or latency data. Although we use the four-parameter log-logistic form for its flexibility, simpler nested models (3PL, with one asymptote fixed; 2PL, with both asymptotes fixed at 0 and 1) or alternative monotonic dose-response functions (e.g., Weibull, log-normal) can be implemented within the same general framework [@ritz_drc_2015; @ritz_streibig_2008]. However, assumptions built into simpler models can affect TDT/TLS parameter estimation.

# Deriving key thermal load sensitivity parameters

The TDT/TLS frameworks are elegant in that they derive metrics with clear biological interpretation that can be compared across populations and species (e.g., *z*, $CT_{max_{1hr}}$). Here, we show that we can derive these same metrics directly from @eq-4pl.

As indicated above, we can model changes in the mean of any of the four parameters in @eq-4pl. Thermal sensitivity (*z*) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ can be derived by modelling the effect of temperature on midpoints specifically ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]c), as follows:

$$
\text{mid} = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,(T - \bar{T}),
$$ {#eq-mid-temp}

where $\text{mid}$ is the $\log_{10}$-time at which survival equals the midpoint between the lower and upper asymptotes at assay temperature $T$ (°C); $\beta_0$ is the intercept giving the midpoint at the grand mean temperature $\bar{T}$; $\beta_1$ is the slope describing how the midpoint changes per °C increase in $T$; and $\bar{T}$ is the grand mean of assay temperatures used to mean-centre $T$, which decorrelates $\beta_0$ from $\beta_1$ and improves parameter estimation and interpretation [@schielzeth_centering_2010].

When the experimental design estimates upper and lower limits of the response (i.e., $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$) near 0 and 1, the 4PL midpoint is equivalent to the classical $t_{50}$. In this case, @eq-mid-temp directly gives $z = -1/\beta_1$, and the critical thermal limit at any reference time $t_\text{ref}$ is $CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) = \bar{T} + (\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0)/\beta_1$ (with $t_\text{ref} = 60$ min for $CT_{max_{1hr}}$).

More generally, the 4PL distinguishes between two related threshold definitions ([@fig-joint-4pl-conceptual]c). A *relative* threshold is defined by each curve's own midpoint, $p = (\text{up}+\text{low})/2$. On this scale, $\text{mid}(T)$ is exactly the relative $\log_{10}t_{50}$, so $z_\text{rel} = -1/\beta_1$ and $CT_{max,\text{rel}}$ is read directly from @eq-mid-temp. An *absolute* threshold instead asks when survival reaches a fixed probability, such as 50% survival, regardless of the fitted asymptotes. When the $\text{low}$ and $\text{up}$ asymptotes differ from 0 and 1, this absolute threshold no longer coincides with the midpoint and requires an asymmetry correction (see [Supplement Section 11](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#derivation-of-the-correction-factors-for-threshold-summaries)):

$$
\log_{10} t_{50}(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-t50}

The corresponding absolute $CT_{max}$ is obtained by applying the same correction on the temperature scale:

$$
CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) \;=\; \bar{T} + \frac{\log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \beta_0}{\beta_1} \;-\; \frac{1}{\beta_1}\cdot\tfrac{1}{k}\log\!\tfrac{\text{up} - 0.5}{0.5 - \text{low}}.
$$ {#eq-ctmax-corrected}

This distinction matters most when asymptotes or curve steepness vary with temperature. If the correction is constant across temperatures, absolute and relative $z$ are the same, although $CT_{max}$ may shift. If the correction changes with temperature, absolute $\log_{10}t_{50}$ is no longer parallel to the midpoint line, and $z_\text{abs}$ can differ from $z_\text{rel}$.

The same logic applies to any fixed mortality threshold. For example, $\log_{10}t_{10}(T)$ is the time to 10% mortality (90% survival) and can identify the onset of population-level mortality, whereas $\log_{10}t_{90}(T)$ is the time to 90% mortality (10% survival) and is closer to population collapse. Any mortality threshold can be estimated from the same posterior by using the corresponding probability in the correction term:

$$
\log_{10} t_m(T) \;=\; \text{mid}(T) \;+\; \frac{1}{k}\,\log\!\frac{\text{up} - (1-m/100)}{(1-m/100) - \text{low}},
$$ {#eq-LTx}

Here, $m$ is the mortality percentage and $1-m/100$ is the corresponding survival probability used in the 4PL. Relative thresholds are useful when differences in asymptotes reflect baseline survival or incomplete response ranges. In contrast, absolute thresholds are useful when the biological question concerns a fixed survival or mortality probability. We therefore recommend reporting which threshold definition was used and, where asymptotes differ strongly among groups or temperatures, checking whether conclusions change under both definitions. In practice, there will often be little difference between relative and absolute summaries (i.e., *z* and $CT_{max}$), but this distinction is important nonetheless.

Equivalently, the model can be reparameterised to estimate *z* and $CT_{max}$ directly. Inverting the relations above ($\beta_1 = -1/z$ and $\beta_0 = \log_{10} t_\text{ref} + (CT_{max} - \bar{T})/z$) reparamaterises the midpoint as $\text{mid}(T) = \log_{10} t_\text{ref} + \bigl(CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) - T\bigr)/z$, which substituted into @eq-4pl gives

$$
p \;=\; \text{low} \;+\; \frac{\text{up} - \text{low}}{1 + \exp\!\left(k\,\left[\log_{10} t - \log_{10} t_\text{ref} - \tfrac{CT_{max}(t_\text{ref}) - T}{z}\right]\right)}.
$$ {#eq-4pl-direct}

Now *z* (estimated on the log scale so it stays positive) and $CT_{max}$ are estimated parameters in their own right, rather than quantities recovered from @eq-mid-temp after fitting. @eq-4pl-direct uses the relative threshold (each curve's own midpoint); an absolute threshold simply subtracts the correction of @eq-ctmax-corrected. Again, temperature effects (and any interactions with moderators) should be included on $\text{low}$, $\text{up}$, and $k$ to correctly estimate absolute thresholds, but are only needed on $\text{mid}$ for relative thresholds.

A powerful aspect of the modelling approach described above is that uncertainty in all estimated quantities can be carried through the full analysis. Each parameter in the model, including *z* and $CT_{max}$ under the direct parameterisation in @eq-4pl-direct, can be estimated separately for biologically meaningful strata (e.g., sex, population, species, treatment) by adding covariates and, where appropriate, random effects to the relevant parameter predictors (see @eq-empirical-direct below). This makes group contrasts and variance decomposition part of the model rather than a post-hoc comparison of fitted curves. We fit the examples below in a Bayesian framework because posterior draws make uncertainty propagation straightforward, but the same likelihoods can also be fit in a frequentist framework, with uncertainty propagated by the Delta method or parametric bootstrap.

# Predicting heat injury and survival under dynamic thermal environments

A particularly powerful aspect of TDT/TLS models are their ability to translate dynamic temperature times series from nature to predict heat injury (HI) accumulation and thus population survival probability [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @li2023; @orsted_finding_2022; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. Heat injury accumulates as the time spent at a given temperature divided by the median lethal time at that temperature, integrated across the temperature trajectory $T(i)$ as follows:

$$
HI(t) \;=\; 100 \int_0^t 10^{\,\big(T(i)\,-\,CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}\big)/z} \,di,
$$ {#eq-hi}

where $z$ describes thermal sensitivity and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$ is the static temperature at which 50% of the population reaches the assayed endpoint after one hour of exposure. Larger $z$ values indicate that a larger temperature increase is needed to reduce tolerance time by a factor of 10, so they correspond to lower sensitivity of tolerance time to warming. When $HI(t) = 100\%$, the cumulative dose equals one $t_{50}$ at the 1-hour reference, predicting 50% mortality (or 50% loss of the assayed sublethal endpoint, e.g.\ fertility; @orsted_suzukii_2024). Injury accumulates only when $T(i)$ exceeds a critical temperature $T_{crit}$ below which damage is repaired as fast as it accrues [@jorgensen_unifying_2021; @orsted_finding_2022]. When repair is included, we model it with a Sharpe-Schoolfield thermal-performance kernel, following @arnold_thermal_2025, which treats repair as temperature dependent, highest near an intermediate optimum, and reduced at colder or hotter temperatures. Field temperature records are typically available at hourly resolution. As such, @eq-hi can be evaluated by summing $100 \cdot 10^{(T_i - CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}})/z}$ across each hourly interval whose temperature $T_i$ exceeds $T_{crit}$, following the implementations in @jorgensen_unifying_2021, @orsted_suzukii_2024, and @li2023. To propagate uncertainty, posterior draws of $z$ and $CT_{max_{1\text{hr}}}$ from the Bayesian dose-response model can be substituted into @eq-hi to calculate $HI(t)$ through time.

A more natural prediction to make may instead be the population-level survival probability over time [@rezende_landscapes_2014; @rezende_drosophila_mortality_2020]. This has the advantage of allowing for a direct connection to population dynamical models which typically use survival, growth and fecundity as vital rates for population-level predictions [@noble2026]. To predict survival, we can calculate the *effective exposure time* at a chosen reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$. More effective time is accumulated at hot temperatures and less at cool ones, in proportion to the scaling factor $10^{(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}$,

$$
\tau_{\text{eff}}(t) \;=\; \int_0^t 10^{\,(T(i) - T_{\text{ref}})/z}\,di,
$$ {#eq-tau-eff}

We can then evaluate the 4PL surface once at the cumulative effective exposure to obtain the predicted population-level survival fraction at every point in the trajectory:

$$
S_{\text{pred}}(t) \;=\; S\!\big(T_{\text{ref}},\, \tau_{\text{eff}}(t)\big).
$$ {#eq-S-pred}

Here $S(T, \tau)$ is the survival surface fitted by the dose-response model, so $S_{\text{pred}}(t)$ is the survival probability for an organism held at *constant* $T_{\text{ref}}$ after exposure duration $\tau_{\text{eff}}(t)$.

# Simulations - Evaluating statistical properties of the joint and two-stage modelling approaches

We assessed the performance of the 4PL and two-stage approaches by estimating bias and 95% coverage for *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ across simulation replicates. Bias was calculated as the estimated value minus the true value, whereas coverage was defined as the proportion of intervals containing the true parameter value. The 4PL approach estimated parameter uncertainty directly from the fitted model. In contrast, the two-stage approach required uncertainty to be propagated from the Stage-2 linear model using the Delta method, a first-order Taylor approximation for nonlinear functions of estimated parameters [@bolker_ecologicalmodels_2008; @lappi2018]. For the two-stage estimates, we constructed intervals using both normal and *t*-distribution quantiles to evaluate the sensitivity of coverage to different assumptions about sampling uncertainty.

We tested the performance of the two-stage and 4PL approaches across scenarios designed to separate different sources of estimator bias and uncertainty. First, we simulated a strict-equivalence baseline in which survival spanned the full 0–1 range, there was no overdispersion, and only the dose-response midpoint varied with temperature. This scenario tests whether the two approaches recover the same quantities when the assumptions of the classical two-stage model are met. Second, we introduced beta-binomial overdispersion while keeping the same underlying dose-response shape, allowing us to isolate the cost of likelihood misspecification when binomial models are applied to overdispersed count data. Third, we simulated departures from the classical dose-response assumptions by allowing the upper asymptote to decline with temperature, both asymptotes to compress with temperature, or the curve steepness to change with temperature. These scenarios reflect empirical cases in which survival or sublethal responses do not span the full response range, or where the shape of the dose-response curve changes across assay temperatures. Finally, we ran sensitivity analyses that varied the strength of upper-asymptote drift, the overall level of the upper asymptote, and different experimental designs.

We also evaluated how different experimental designs impact estimates by simulating response curves from several static temperatures and exposure durations with modest numbers of individuals, samples, or replicate vials per treatment [@truebano2018; @jorgensen_drosophila_2019; @li2023; @orsted_suzukii_2024; @faber_tdt_plants_2024]. In the simulations, designs included one to five replicate cups per temperature-by-time treatment, with 10 to 20 organisms per cup sampled from a uniform distribution. We also explored experimental designs that conducted experiments over short time spans across the different temperatures which we expected should impact model fit and therefore *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$. For each scenario-by-design combination, we simulated 1,000 datasets and fit both the two-stage (both relative and absolute summaries) and 4PL approaches. Overall, this amounted to 29,000 simulations across the 29 scenario-by-design cells. We report Monte Carlo standard errors (MCSE) for the bias and coverage summaries to better understand across simulation uncertainty [@williams2024; @direnzo2023; @koehler2009].

#### *Simulation results*

When the data-generating process matched the classical assumptions, with survival spanning the full 0-1 range and temperature affecting only the dose-response midpoint, the two-stage approach performed similarly to the joint 4PL model, producing little bias and near-nominal coverage when confidence intervals were constructed with *t*-distribution quantiles. This distinction was important: using normal (z) quantiles instead produced anti-conservative intervals with coverage well below the nominal 95% level (@fig-sims & [Supplement 8.1](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#strict-equivalence-baseline) & [8.2](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#likelihood-misspecification-alone)).

In contrast, the two-stage approach resulted in biased *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$, often with poor coverage, when temperature affected the upper asymptote (`up`) or the rate of decline (`k`) (@fig-sims). These biases were consistent even with larger sample sizes (@fig-sims), but they became most consequential under exactly the kinds of designs often used in practice: sparse temperature-duration grids, short exposure windows, and low replication (@fig-sim-design). The joint 4PL remained near-unbiased and well-calibrated across these designs, whereas two-stage bias grew and interval coverage collapsed as grids became sparser, replication fell, or exposure windows shortened — most severely at the shortest exposure cap, where two-stage coverage of *z* fell below 0.05 even with the *t*-correction (@fig-sim-design; full per-cell detail in [Supplement 8.6](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#experimental-design-impacts)). Bias for both *z* and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ from the two-stage approaches was particularly severe as the upper survival threshold decreased (@fig-sims2).

```{r}
#| label: fig-sims
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7.5
#| fig-cap: "Simulation performance when the data-generating process violates classical TDT shape assumptions. Row labelled **'up varies with T'**: upper asymptote $\\text{up}$ declines 0.01 per °C from 0.92 at $T=34$ °C, with $\\text{low}=0.05$ and $k=8$ constant in $T$. Row labelled **'k varies with T'**: slope $k$ ranges from 6 at 30 °C to 10 at 38 °C, with $\\text{up}=0.92$ and $\\text{low}=0.05$ constant. Both rows use 5 replicate cups per (T × duration) cell, $N_{sim}=1000$ simulated datasets per row, and beta-binomial overdispersion ($\\phi=5$). Panel a): mean signed bias in $z$ (left column) and $CT_{max,1h}$ (right column) with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE). Panel b): 95% interval coverage with the same column layout. Dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal coverage. Absolute-scale methods (`joint_4pl_abs` and all two-stage variants) are evaluated against the analytical absolute-50% truth; `joint_4pl` is evaluated against the relative midpoint truth. Two-stage normal- and t-quantile variants share point estimates and differ only in Stage-2 delta-method interval construction."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + paired colour palette (matches notes writeup) --
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bin_t",
                    "two_stage_bb",  "two_stage_bb_t",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)", "Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)",  "Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)",      "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE) *
                       (1 - mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE)) / dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

# Use n_reps = 5 cells of Scen 3 and Scen 5 for the manuscript figure.
.d <- dplyr::bind_rows(.load_screened("scen3"),
                       .load_screened("scen5")) |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("_n5$", scenario)) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                labels = .method_labels),
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                labels = c("z (°C)",
                                           "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    scenario_label = dplyr::recode(
      sub("_n5$", "", scenario),
      scen3_heat_lowers_u = "up varies with T",
      scen5_sharpen       = "k varies with T"),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,
    cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov
  )

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.p_bias <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = mean_bias, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL,
       title = "a) Bias") +
  .theme_proj + theme(legend.position = "none")

.p_cov <- ggplot(.d, aes(x = coverage, y = method, colour = method)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = 0.25) +
  geom_point(size = 2.4) +
  facet_grid(scenario_label ~ quantity) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, 0.5, 0.95)) +
  labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL,
       title = "b) Coverage") +
  .theme_proj

.p_bias / .p_cov + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-sims2
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-cap: "Bias in $z$ and $CT_{max,1h}$ as the partial-mortality regime is swept along two orthogonal axes. Row labelled **'up drifts with T (varying $\\beta_{up}$)'**: upper asymptote at $T = \\bar T$ fixed at $\\text{up}_0 = 0.92$, slope $\\beta_{up}$ varied across $\\{-0.005, -0.010, -0.015, -0.019\\}$ percentage points per °C, so $\\text{up}$ stays high at cool assays and declines progressively at hotter ones. Row labelled **'up is constant but reduced (varying $\\text{up}_0$)'**: $\\beta_{up} = 0$ (no temperature drift); the baseline level itself is swept across $\\{0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99\\}$, representing a constant fraction of the cohort that is never susceptible regardless of temperature. Both rows: $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, mid linear in $T$ with slope $-0.15$, beta-binomial likelihood ($\\phi=5$), $n_{reps}=5$, $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. Points are mean signed bias with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE); dashed lines mark zero bias. Left column: $z$; right column: $CT_{max,1h}$. Only the four distinct point-estimate methods are shown: the two-stage Normal-CI and t-CI variants share point estimates within each Stage-1 family, so bias is identical between them; coverage differences appear in the supplementary writeup. The two failure signatures differ: drifting $\\text{up}$ tilts the Stage-2 slope (large $z$ bias, modest $CT_{max,1h}$ bias); constant low $\\text{up}$ shifts the Stage-2 intercept (large $CT_{max,1h}$ bias, with $z$ bias of opposite sign growing as $\\text{up}_0$ falls)."

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(here)
library(patchwork)

# ---- Method ordering + colour palette ---------------------------------
# Bias is identical for the Normal-CI and t-CI variants of each
# two-stage Stage-1 family (they share point estimates; only Stage-2 CI
# construction differs). For this bias-only figure we therefore plot
# just the four distinct point-estimate methods: two_stage_bin,
# two_stage_bb, joint_4pl (relative), joint_4pl_abs (absolute).
.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin", "two_stage_bb",
                    "joint_4pl",     "joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin", "Two-stage BB",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)", "Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f", "#a63603", "#006d2c", "#a1d99b"),
                         .method_labels)

# ---- Two-truth constants (mid line is fixed across all 25 sim cells) --
.TRUTH_REL <- c(z = -1 / -0.15,
                CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

# ---- BB Stage-1 pathology screening + two-truth aggregation -----------
.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(
    here::here("output", "sim_twostage"),
    pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
    full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))

  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z"         & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |>
    dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)

  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario", "sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb", "two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                         .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)],
                         truth),
      bias    = estimate - truth_use,
      covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper
    ) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(
      n         = dplyr::n(),
      mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
      mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
      .groups   = "drop"
    )
}

.scen6 <- .load_screened("scen6") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = -as.numeric(sub("scen6_ub_m", "", scenario)) / 1000,
                sweep_label = "up drifts with T (varying beta_up, percentage points / degC)")

.scen7 <- .load_screened("scen7") |>
  dplyr::mutate(sweep_x = as.numeric(sub("scen7_u0_", "", scenario)) / 100,
                sweep_label = "up is constant but reduced (varying up_0)")

.shape_fmt <- function(d) {
  d |>
    dplyr::filter(method %in% .method_levels) |>
    dplyr::mutate(
      method   = factor(method,   levels = .method_levels,
                                  labels = .method_labels),
      quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z", "CTmax_1hr"),
                                  labels = c("z (°C)",
                                             "CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
      bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias,
      bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias
    )
}

.theme_proj <- theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(),
        strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
        legend.position  = "bottom",
        legend.title     = element_blank(),
        legend.text      = element_text(size = 9),
        panel.spacing    = unit(0.8, "lines"))

.build <- function(d, x_label, x_breaks) {
  ggplot(d, aes(x = sweep_x, y = mean_bias, colour = method)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = bias_lo, ymax = bias_hi), width = 0) +
    geom_point(size = 2.4) +
    geom_line() +
    facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = x_breaks) +
    labs(x = x_label, y = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)") +
    .theme_proj
}

.p6 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen6),
              x_label  = expression(beta[up]~"(percentage points / °C, drift of "*plain(up)*" with T)"),
              x_breaks = c(-0.019, -0.015, -0.010, -0.005)) +
  ggtitle(expression("a) " * plain(up) * " drifts with T (varying " * beta[up] * ")"))

.p7 <- .build(.shape_fmt(.scen7),
              x_label  = expression(up[0]~"(constant upper asymptote, all T)"),
              x_breaks = c(0.65, 0.75, 0.85, 0.95, 0.99)) +
  ggtitle("b) up is constant but reduced (varying up_0)")

.p6 / .p7 + plot_layout(guides = "collect", heights = c(1, 1)) &
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-sim-design
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-cap: "Estimator performance under information-limited experimental designs — the conditions most common in real thermal-tolerance datasets. Panels a) and b) vary experimental design and replication: a full (5 temperatures × 6 durations) versus sparse (3 × 4) temperature–duration grid, crossed with 1, 3, or 5 replicate cups per cell. Panels c) and d) vary the maximum exposure duration (the longest assay window: 60, 120, 240, or 405 min) on the full grid with five replicates. Panels a) and c) show mean signed bias and panels b) and d) show 95% interval coverage for thermal sensitivity $z$ (left) and $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ (right). Points are Monte Carlo means with 95% Monte Carlo uncertainty intervals (mean ± 1.96 MCSE; bias MCSE: $\\text{SD}/\\sqrt{N}$; coverage MCSE: $\\sqrt{p(1-p)/N}$); dashed lines mark zero bias and nominal 0.95 coverage. Bias panels show the four distinct point estimators (the two-stage Normal- and *t*-CI variants share point estimates); coverage panels additionally distinguish the *t*-CI variants. All cells use a beta-binomial data-generating process ($\\phi=5$) with a fixed 4PL shape ($\\text{up}=0.92$, $\\text{low}=0.05$, $k=8$, midpoint linear in $T$); $N_{sim}=1000$ datasets per cell. The joint 4PL stays near-unbiased with near-nominal coverage across every design, whereas the two-stage estimators degrade sharply as designs become sparse, under-replicated, or short — most extremely at the 60-min cap, where two-stage coverage of $z$ falls below 0.05 even with the *t*-correction while the joint 4PL holds near 0.95. Full per-cell detail and RMSE are in the supplement."

# Combined experimental-design figure (scen8 = design × replication; scen9 =
# maximum exposure duration). Reads the per-simulation aggregates only — no
# model fits here. Mirrors the screening/aggregation used by the other
# simulation figures.
library(dplyr); library(tidyr); library(ggplot2); library(here); library(patchwork)

.method_levels <- c("two_stage_bin","two_stage_bin_t","two_stage_bb",
                    "two_stage_bb_t","joint_4pl","joint_4pl_abs")
.method_labels <- c("Two-stage bin (Normal CI)","Two-stage bin (t CI)",
                    "Two-stage BB (Normal CI)","Two-stage BB (t CI)",
                    "Joint 4PL (relative)","Joint 4PL (absolute)")
.method_cols <- setNames(c("#54278f","#cbc9e2","#a63603","#fdae6b",
                           "#006d2c","#a1d99b"), .method_labels)
.point_codes <- c("two_stage_bin","two_stage_bb","joint_4pl","joint_4pl_abs")
.TRUTH_REL   <- c(z = -1 / -0.15, CTmax_1hr = 34 + (log10(60) - 1.5) / -0.15)

.load_screened <- function(prefix) {
  files <- list.files(here::here("output","sim_twostage"),
                      pattern = paste0("^per_sim_", prefix, "_.*[.]rds$"),
                      full.names = TRUE)
  ps <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(files, readRDS))
  bad <- ps |>
    dplyr::filter(method == "two_stage_bb",
                  (quantity == "z" & abs(bias) > 20) |
                  (quantity == "CTmax_1hr" & abs(bias) > 10)) |>
    dplyr::distinct(scenario, sim_id) |> dplyr::mutate(bb_path = TRUE)
  ps |>
    dplyr::left_join(bad, by = c("scenario","sim_id")) |>
    dplyr::filter(is.na(bb_path) |
                  !(method %in% c("two_stage_bb","two_stage_bb_t"))) |>
    dplyr::select(-bb_path) |>
    dplyr::mutate(truth_use = ifelse(method == "joint_4pl",
                                     .TRUTH_REL[as.character(quantity)], truth),
                  bias = estimate - truth_use,
                  covered = is.finite(lower) & is.finite(upper) &
                            lower <= truth_use & truth_use <= upper) |>
    dplyr::filter(success) |>
    dplyr::group_by(scenario, method, quantity) |>
    dplyr::summarise(n = dplyr::n(),
                     mean_bias = mean(bias, na.rm = TRUE),
                     mcse_bias = stats::sd(bias, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(dplyr::n()),
                     coverage  = mean(covered, na.rm = TRUE),
                     mcse_cov  = sqrt(mean(covered) * (1 - mean(covered)) / dplyr::n()),
                     .groups = "drop") |>
    dplyr::mutate(method_code = as.character(method))
}

.fmt <- function(d) d |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    quantity = factor(quantity, levels = c("z","CTmax_1hr"),
                      labels = c("z (°C)","CT[max,1h] (°C)")),
    method_lab = factor(.method_labels[match(method_code, .method_levels)],
                        levels = .method_labels),
    bias_lo = mean_bias - 1.96 * mcse_bias, bias_hi = mean_bias + 1.96 * mcse_bias,
    cov_lo  = coverage  - 1.96 * mcse_cov,  cov_hi  = coverage  + 1.96 * mcse_cov)

.d8 <- .fmt(.load_screened("scen8")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(design = ifelse(grepl("full", scenario), "full", "sparse"),
                nrep = as.integer(sub(".*_n([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                cell = factor(sprintf("%s × %d", design, nrep),
                              levels = rev(c("sparse × 1","full × 1","sparse × 3",
                                             "full × 3","sparse × 5","full × 5"))))
.d9 <- .fmt(.load_screened("scen9")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(tmax = as.integer(sub(".*_tmax_([0-9]+)$", "\\1", scenario)),
                cell = factor(sprintf("%d min", tmax),
                              levels = rev(c("60 min","120 min","240 min","405 min"))))

.qlab <- as_labeller(c(`z (°C)` = "italic(z)~(degree*C)",
                       `CT[max,1h] (°C)` = "CT[max[1*hr]]~(degree*C)"), label_parsed)
.thm <- theme_classic(base_size = 10) +
  theme(strip.background = element_blank(), strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11), legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.title = element_blank(), legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
        panel.spacing = unit(0.6, "lines"))
.scol <- scale_colour_manual(values = .method_cols, breaks = .method_labels,
                             limits = .method_labels, drop = FALSE)

.bias_panel <- function(d, title)
  ggplot(dplyr::filter(d, method_code %in% .point_codes),
         aes(mean_bias, cell, colour = method_lab, group = method_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = bias_lo, xmax = bias_hi), height = .3,
                 position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.7, position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, scales = "free_x", labeller = .qlab) +
  .scol + guides(colour = "none") +
  labs(x = "Mean bias (estimate - truth)", y = NULL, title = title) + .thm
.cov_panel <- function(d, title)
  ggplot(d, aes(coverage, cell, colour = method_lab, group = method_lab)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = .95, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = cov_lo, xmax = cov_hi), height = .3,
                 position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  geom_point(size = 1.7, position = position_dodge(.6)) +
  facet_wrap(~ quantity, labeller = .qlab) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = c(0, .5, .95)) +
  .scol + guides(colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, byrow = TRUE,
                                       override.aes = list(size = 2.6))) +
  labs(x = "95% CI coverage", y = NULL, title = title) + .thm

.fig_design <-
  (.bias_panel(.d8, "a) Design × replication — bias") |
   .cov_panel(.d8,  "b) Design × replication — coverage")) /
  (.bias_panel(.d9, "c) Maximum exposure duration — bias") |
   .cov_panel(.d9,  "d) Maximum exposure duration — coverage")) +
  plot_layout(guides = "collect") & theme(legend.position = "bottom")

dir.create(here::here("output","figs"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
ggsave(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.png"), .fig_design,
       width = 11, height = 8.5, dpi = 600, bg = "white")
ggsave(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.svg"), .fig_design,
       width = 11, height = 8.5, bg = "white")
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output","figs","fig_sim_design.png"))
```

# Worked examples

We applied the 4PL framework to four case studies spanning lethal counts, continuous-proportion physiological data and sublethal responses: three cereal-aphid species [@li2023], snow gum leaf PSII function [@arnold_psii_2026], zebrafish larvae under contrasting oxygen levels [@saruhashi2026] and vinegar fly mortality, heat coma and productivity [@orsted_suzukii_2024]. These examples show how the same fitted response surface can estimate $z$, $CT_{max}$ and, for lethal endpoints, $T_{crit}$, compare groups, and propagate uncertainty into heat-injury and survival predictions. The workflow is implemented in `bayesTLS`; the online supplement provides installation instructions, a tutorial, and the full model specifications ([Supplement Section 2.2](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-empirical-model-specs)).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{mid}_{ij}(T) &= \log_{10} t_{\text{ref}} + \frac{CT_{max,ij}(t_{\text{ref}}) - T_{ij}}{z_{ij}},\\
CT_{max,ij}(t_{\text{ref}}) &= \alpha_0 + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\alpha} + b_j,\\
\log z_{ij} &= \zeta_0 + \mathbf{x}_{ij}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\zeta} + u_j .
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-empirical-direct}

For the main grouped case studies, we used the direct parameterisation in @eq-empirical-direct, placing species, oxygen treatment or recovery condition directly on $CT_{max}$ and $z$. This lets group-specific thermal limits and sensitivities fall straight out of the posterior. The vinegar-fly sex comparison was fit in the equivalent midpoint form, with `sex` and its temperature interaction on $\text{mid}$. Count responses were fit with beta-binomial likelihoods, while snow gum PSII proportions were fit with a Beta likelihood. We fitted the manuscript examples with the Bayesian implementation, using `brms` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("brms"))`; @burkner_brms_2017) and Stan (v`r tryCatch(cmdstanr::cmdstan_version(), error = function(e) "2.38.0")`; @carpenter2017) through `bayesTLS` (v`r as.character(packageVersion("bayesTLS"))`); equivalent frequentist fits are available through `freqTLS` and should give similar point estimates for the same likelihood and parameterisation.

For lethal aphid and vinegar-fly fits, posterior draws were passed to `predict_heat_injury()` to predict heat injury and survival under field or field-derived temperature traces, with and without an illustrative temperature-dependent repair function. Full formulas, priors, sampler controls, diagnostics and temperature-trace details are reported in the [online supplement](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-empirical-model-specs).

```{r}
#| label: aphid-hi-facts
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false
# Code-generated aphid heat-injury facts, read from the supplement's cached
# figure data (output/data/fig5_hi_data.rds) when present, with a fallback so
# this manuscript renders independently of the supplement (matching this
# project's independent-render architecture).
.f5 <- tryCatch(readRDS(here::here("output", "data", "fig5_hi_data.rds")),
                error = function(e) NULL)
.f5_fresh <- !is.null(.f5$aphid_Tc)
# Per-species relative-threshold T_crit (range across the three aphid species).
aphid_Tc_rng <- if (.f5_fresh) range(.f5$aphid_Tc$Tc) else c(23.8, 28.2)
# End-of-season predicted survival without repair (range across species).
aphid_surv_end <- if (.f5_fresh) {
  .f5$aphid |>
    dplyr::filter(repair == "No repair") |>
    dplyr::group_by(species) |>
    dplyr::slice_max(time, n = 1) |>
    dplyr::pull(surv_med)
} else c(0.008, 0.019)
```

The heat-injury forecasts use the relative-threshold $T_{crit}$ from each lethal fit and the same posterior draws that estimate $z$ and $CT_{max}$. This keeps uncertainty linked across static thermal-tolerance estimates and dynamic survival predictions.

## Cereal aphids (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*)

Comparing three cereal-aphid species in a single joint 4PL recovered the thermal-tolerance ordering reported by @li2023: *M. dirhodum* was the least heat-sensitive (largest $z$) and *R. padi* the most heat-tolerant (highest $CT_{max_{1hr}}$), with all three estimates falling inside the ranges Li et al. report ([@fig-tls-comparison]a). Projected onto the unmodified Wuhan May–June trace, cumulative heat injury rose once temperatures exceeded each species' $T_{crit}$: without repair, predicted survival fell to roughly `r round(100 * min(aphid_surv_end))`–`r round(100 * max(aphid_surv_end))`% by late June, while the illustrative repair model kept the two more heat-tolerant species (*R. padi*, *S. avenae*) largely protected over this window but could not rescue the most vulnerable *M. dirhodum* (lowest $T_{crit}$; [@fig-heat-injury]d-f).

## Snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*)

The snow gum case study demonstrates the same modelling framework for continuous-proportion data, here for photosystem II function measured from chlorophyll fluorescence. Photosystem II function had the largest $z$ among the four case studies, indicating the shallowest decline in tolerance time with warming ([@fig-tls-comparison]b), and illustrating how the 4PL can be used for sublethal physiological endpoints as well as mortality counts.

## Zebrafish (*Danio rerio*)

The zebrafish case study shows how the model can compare experimental treatments in a single joint analysis while testing a mechanistic prediction. Larvae assayed under hyperoxia reached a higher $CT_{max_{1hr}}$ than under normoxia (`r zf_ctmax_hyper` vs `r zf_ctmax_norm` °C), consistent with the oxygen- and capacity-limited thermal tolerance (OCLTT) prediction that greater oxygen availability raises the upper thermal limit ([@fig-tls-comparison]c).

## Vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*)

The vinegar fly case study shows how the same framework can be used for sex-specific mortality and downstream heat-injury prediction. Our re-analysis of the mortality data from @orsted_suzukii_2024 recovered similar $z$ and $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ estimates for males and females, consistent with their reported values (Table 1: $z$ = 3.28 for males and 3.03 for females; $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ = 35.2 °C for both sexes). We found no sex difference in either quantity, and relative and absolute $z$ and $CT_{max_{4hr}}$ gave similar estimates in this example ([@fig-tls-comparison]d). We also apply our approach to sublethal thermal-response traits in the supplement — the [time-to-coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [reproductive output](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses — with our values closely matching those of @orsted_suzukii_2024.

For lethal measures, without repair, cumulative heat injury exceeded one $LT_{50}$ dose and predicted survival declined sharply during the warmest part of the modelled microclimate trace. Incorporating a repair model reduced heat injury and offset predicted mortality, showing how the same fitted 4PL can be combined with alternative assumptions about damage repair ([@fig-heat-injury]a-c).

```{r}
#| label: fig-tls-comparison
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Thermal sensitivity ($z$, left of each panel) and the critical thermal limit ($CT_{max}$, right) estimated by the joint Bayesian four-parameter logistic (4PL) model for four case studies: a) three cereal-aphid species (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*), b) snow gum (*Eucalyptus pauciflora*) leaf photosystem II function under dark vs 90-min-light post-heat recovery, c) zebrafish (*Danio rerio*) larvae under normoxia vs hyperoxia, and d) vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*) by sex. Larger $z$ values indicate lower sensitivity of tolerance time to warming. Filled ridges are posterior densities, coloured by group. The black-outlined point and bar give the posterior median and 95% credible interval, which are also printed beside each distribution. The $CT_{max}$ reference exposure follows each study's own design — 1 hour for aphids, leaf PSII, and zebrafish, and 4 hours for the vinegar fly. Within each panel the groups were fit within a single joint model, so group differences are evaluated as pairwise posterior contrasts, annotated as brackets labelled with $p_{\\mathrm{MCMC}}$ (twice the smaller posterior tail probability of the difference; smaller values indicate stronger evidence that the groups differ)."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig4_case_study_densities.png"))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-heat-injury
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Heat-injury accumulation and predicted survival under field temperature trajectories for the vinegar fly (*Drosophila suzukii*, by sex; panels a-c) and three cereal aphids (*Metopolophium dirhodum*, *Sitobion avenae*, *Rhopalosiphum padi*; panels d-f). (a, d) the temperature trace — shaded NicheMapR microclimate temperature for the fly (Rennes, France, Jun–Aug 2018; @orsted_suzukii_2024 Zenodo record 10821572) and the unmodified hourly air-temperature trace for the aphids (Wuhan, May–June 2016; @li2023, Open-Meteo ERA5) — with the relative-threshold $T_{crit}$ (dotted; per species for the aphids). (b, e) cumulative heat injury (100% = one $LT_{50}$ dose, dotted grey) and (c, f) predicted survival, each with and without damage repair (line type). Fly panels are coloured by sex, aphid panels by species. Bands are ±1 SE across posterior draws. Repair uses an illustrative Sharpe–Schoolfield kernel with $r_{ref}=0.03$ LT50-dose h$^{-1}$ at the reference temperature; it is not empirically fitted."
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("output", "figs", "fig5_heat_injury.png"))
```


# Discussion

We have shown that the Bayesian 4PL dose-response model provides a flexible and statistically rigorous framework for estimating thermal tolerance and thermal sensitivity. Simulations show that the model is robust across diverse experimental designs and data conditions, while the case studies recover familiar TLS quantities, with posterior estimates of $z$ falling within the range typically reported for thermal tolerance landscapes (approximately 2-7 °C; @rezende_landscapes_2014). The strongest practical message from the simulations is that two-stage analyses become risky when experiments are sparse, replication is low, exposure windows are short, or the survival-curve bounds (`low`, `up`) depart from 0 and 1 [@lappi2018]. The framework also extends naturally beyond lethal count data to sublethal and non-binomial endpoints, including time-to-event responses, mixture models that separate response and non-response components, and Gamma-distributed responses, while propagating uncertainty into group contrasts and heat-injury predictions. We develop these extensions in the online supplement, including the vinegar-fly [coma](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-coma) and [productivity](https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/#sec-dros-productivity) analyses.

The Bayesian model fits the raw response data in a single hierarchical analysis, accommodating diverse experimental designs while partitioning variation among biological and experimental levels. This opens up extensions that are difficult to implement with a two-stage approach. For example, clones, individuals, tissues, populations, or treatments can be included in the same model to estimate genetic, individual, tissue-specific, or environmental sources of variation in $z$ and $CT_{max}$. The same posterior can also be used to contrast the underlying 4PL parameters themselves (`low`, `up`, `k`, and `mid`), making it possible to identify whether groups differ in baseline survival, maximum attainable survival, curve steepness, or time-to-threshold. Full propagation of sampling uncertainty should also make sensitivity and tolerance estimates more useful for robust meta-analyses, comparative studies, and systems models of biological responses to extreme heat [@noble2026]. Finally, the same framework can be extended to estimate the effects of acclimation, interacting environmental stressors, or other dose-response processes in which biological response depends jointly on stress intensity and exposure duration, including chemical stressors such as herbicides [@arnold_thermal_2025]. More generally, our modelling framework can be extended to any dose-response framework, including toxicology studies where toxicant concentration replaces temperature, enabling new ways to consistently analyse and report sensitivity and tolerance across fields.

Bayesian methods may be more complicated to implement; however, our `bayesTLS` R package should improve accessibility to experimental and comparative biologists. Nonetheless, similar models can also be fit within a likelihood framework and error propagated using parametric bootstrapping or the Delta method [@bolker_ecologicalmodels_2008]. An important future development will be to incorporate real rates of repair from experiments [@arnold_thermal_2025; @orsted2026; @buckley_damage_2025] to improve model predictions in nature. By improving how we estimate thermal sensitivity, thermal tolerance, and their biological drivers across populations and species, this framework strengthens our ability to predict the biological impacts of extreme heat in a warming world.

# Acknowledgements

We thank Johannes Overgaard, Joey Bernhardt, Wilco Verbeck, Jennifer Sunday, Ignacio Peralta-Maraver, Arnaud Sentis and Jeff Arnoldi for discussions during an extreme heat workshop supported by the ClimateCountDown ERC Consolidator Grant, funded by the European Research Council under the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme (Grant agreement no. 101169909). We would also like to thank Gwenaëlle Deconninck, Charlie Cornwallis and Tobias Uller for helpful comments. D.W.A.N was supported by an ARC Future Fellowship (FT220100276) and thanks the ANU, Lund University, the ANU Hansen Scandinavian Friendship Grant and the Royal Physiographic Society of Lund for funding that supported the sabbatical during which this paper was written. P.P. was supported by a Wenner-Gren Stiftelserna postdoctoral fellowship (UPD2024-0239).

# Author contributions {.unnumbered}

D.W.A.N.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.P.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Visualization, Writing – Original Draft. P.A.A.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – Review & Editing. All authors contributed to interpretation of results, reviewed and edited the manuscript, approved the final version, and agree to be accountable for the work.

# Data and code availability {.unnumbered}

All data, analysis scripts, and rendered outputs are available at https://github.com/daniel1noble/bayesTLS. The online supplement, tutorial, and `bayesTLS` installation instructions are available at https://daniel1noble.github.io/bayesTLS/.

# Conflict of interest {.unnumbered}

The authors declare no competing interests.

# AI Declaration
During the preparation of this manuscript, the authors used generative AI tools, including OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex (vers. 5.5) and Claude Code (vers. opus 4.8), to assist with language editing, structural refinement, mathematical derivations, code building and checking, R package construction and formatting. All scientific ideas, analyses, interpretations, and conclusions were developed and verified by the authors. Any AI-assisted output was critically reviewed and edited by the authors, who accept full responsibility for the final content of the manuscript, supplement, code, and associated R package.

# References {.unnumbered}

:::
